transcript: Maturity in motherhood.  Have you heard of it ?  But hold on, let's go deep. For me, maturity in motherhood isn't about finally learning or thinking you know what to do.  It's when you understand that every choice you make as a mother can impact the person your child is becoming, whether it's in their gaze, their boundaries, their relationship with desire, or their relationship with excess.  And perhaps that's why motherhood in 2026 is so challenging, because parenting itself is already one of the greatest challenges of motherhood. But now in 2026 you're trying to educate while it seems like the whole world is almost educating at the same time.  Because we mothers need to learn to deal with, for example, a tendency that arrives before we can even have a conversation with our children, with comparisons that arrive before they are even aware of them, with desires coming before criteria.  And in the midst of all this, we're left here trying to figure out what the right choice is.  And I 've been thinking a lot about the price of choices in motherhood, because for a long time we've associated being a good mother with being able to give of ourselves, or rather, to be able to give ourselves to our children in the sense of being present, providing structure, giving affection, giving opportunities, giving what we ourselves had.  And in my case, it's even more about giving them what I didn't have, because I'm completely clear that I try very hard to compensate my children and give them what I would have liked to have had, perhaps, at their age, and what I fought so hard to have today.  But there's a part of motherhood that nobody romanticizes, and that's understanding very clearly that every choice we make comes at a cost.  Yes , it charges, too much access charges, the limit also charges.  And perhaps maturity in motherhood begins when we stop asking only: "Can I give this to my child?"  And he begins to ask: "How can this help in the kind of person I would like my child to become?"  Because saying yes is tempting, it's easier, it solves the problem immediately, it avoids frustration, it prevents many conflicts, and it also avoids that feeling of being harsh when inside you're trying to do the right thing.  And saying yes also educates.  But sometimes it can educate for excess, it educates for haste, it educates for the idea that every desire needs to become possession, it educates for the feeling that will is urgency.  And perhaps that is one of the challenges of being a mother in today's world.  Actually, that's one of the biggest challenges, right?  Because we're not raising a child in a neutral world.  We're raising children in a world that offers too much, compares too much, accelerates too much, and transforms everything into a necessity.  If we don't filter anything, the world will end up educating our children through excess.  So, for me, maturity in motherhood has a lot to do with editing, with editing the impulse to compensate, editing the guilt, editing the excess, editing what enters our home, editing what our children watch, because all of this will impact their repertoire, their perspective, and the idea of ​​value that they are building.  And in the end, it's not about controlling everything, because that's impossible, but it's about understanding that not everything that's available needs to be included.  Not everything that glitters needs to be bought.  Not everything they want now needs to be delivered now, because the price of limiting access is the frustration of the moment, but the price of unfiltered access can be the lack of discernment later.  Between those two prices, I've really been trying to choose more consciously, knowing that I can obviously be wrong, knowing that many times my choices won't be perfect, because perfection doesn't exist, but trying to choose with less guilt and more honesty.  And today I understand that loving also means knowing how to accept some "no's," it means enduring being temporarily misunderstood.  It's about choosing the right level of discomfort.  It's paying a price now so they can take something later.  Because I certainly don't want my children to be here in the very distant future.  I want them to leave my house with a critical eye, with a repertoire of ideas, with discernment, with the capacity to desire, without being completely controlled by desire.  And the question I leave you with on this Mother's Day is one that I've been asking myself .  Is what I'm offering today shaping a more discerning human being, or is it just solving immediate problems?   It's something to think about, isn't it?  Happy Mother's Day.
